summary: Dan Shapiro argues that startups should embrace polarization in recruiting rather than trying to appeal to everyone. Using the example of founding Ontela, he explains how they deliberately chose to locate on Seattle's Westside despite most tech talent being on the Eastside near Microsoft. This decision alienated over half their potential candidates but made the remaining minority enthusiastic about the opportunity. The strategy proved instrumental in making early hires, including a senior developer who joined largely because the shorter commute meant more time with his kids. The core lesson is that startups should leverage their uniqueness as a feature, not a bug.
key_points: Startups compete against big companies by targeting a passionate minority rather than appealing to the broadest possible talent pool
Ontela deliberately chose Seattle's Westside location despite most tech talent living on the Eastside near Microsoft, turning geography into a recruiting advantage
Polarizing decisions filter for candidates who are genuinely excited rather than merely willing, improving cultural fit and enthusiasm
The first hire was a senior developer who took a big pay cut primarily because the location meant seeing his kids more — a motivation big companies couldn't match
money_quotes: Those companies need to attract armies. You only need to hire one.
It's vastly better to polarize the market, alienate half your potential work force, and buck conventional wisdom than it is to play to the middle.
We don't have to hire most of the technology talent in Seattle. We just have to get a few awesome people.
There's a simple heuristic here that most companies use: the company's world headquarters is the piece of commercial real estate closest to the CEO's house.
Come on over! We have great healthcare benefits! And you can get your soul back if you need it later, promise!
He figured he'd get home sooner to see his kids if he didn't have to battle the bridge.
key_insight: Startups win talent not by competing head-to-head with big companies for the broad market, but by making polarizing choices that deeply resonate with a passionate few.
